This is here for you to know what the player might be trying to achieve in game. ${PLAYER_ACTIONS_THIS_ROUND}
The length of it should MATCH the length of the player’s specific action, plus about 50%. ONLY that one action you are adding details to. So however many words the action made by the player was, plus about 50%. No random details of course, it needs to be immersive, fitting, logical specific policies that you're adding, to increase the chance of a potentially broad player action. Even if the player action is good and detailed, you should refine it. The tone must also emulate it. DO NOT match the length of any of the OTHER actions. Remember, the action to update is: ${DESCRIPTION_ACTION_TEXT}
[Language] Our game supports different languages. Even though this prompt’s instructions and the gamestate details might be in English, your output should be in the language of ${language}. In other words, regardless of whatever language the polities' names, the in-game regions, or the prompt instructions might be in, make sure that anything the user can see is in the language of ${language}. Make sure your output is in understandable grammar and vocabulary depending on the language.
Remember, what you write should enhance and make the player’s written action, more detailed, more entertaining, and easier for the Simulator A.I. to understand. It should be an action that urges/inspires an engaging result. Or a chat with an engaging premise. So ADD content to the player’s action, lots of it, but don’t remove any of the player’s intent.

--- OUTPUT FORMAT (return valid JSON only) ---
Return JSON only:
{"kind":"action","title":"","text":"","invitees":[],"chatStarter":""}
Use kind="chat" ONLY when the player clearly wants negotiation, outreach, or a conference; then "chatStarter" is the player's opening message and "invitees" lists the target polity names. Otherwise kind="action" with empty invitees and chatStarter. Write "title" and "text" in ${language}, preserving the player's tone and intent while adding practical specificity.
Output ONLY the JSON object described above - no prose, no markdown, no code fences.
tasks.eventConsolidator:
I'm making a game where the player makes decisions while roleplaying as a country in a specific historical scenario. The game is a sandbox focused on diplomacy.
 
Your job is to convert a list of events AND a list of chat histories that were once generated by a simulator A.I. that actually simulated the game, into a consolidated summary, try your best for it to be no more than 360 Words, but it can be a bit longer depending on the situation. 
 
DO NOT INCLUDE ANY WORD COUNTS IN YOUR CONSOLIDATION! JUST ADHERE TO THE RULE.
 
The game is a turn based strategy game set in ${ORIGIN_ROUND_DATE}
The player is playing as ${PLAYER_POLITY}
 
In our game, there is a map of many, many regions, and many polities, most of which own some regions. This means you should prioritize and clarify the Region/Map Feature/General Map changes in the events list above, in your summary. In other words, your summary should be a brief history of this custom timeline in our game, for future rounds A.I. Simulation of the game.
 
You should also note that some events in our game can be simulated with great detail and specificity. In other words, in the event list there will occasionally be Extra Detailed Events that occur between chunks of regular events. Those types of events begin on the day of the event displayed right before the detailed event. So keep that in mind.
 
Here is the list of past events, you must summarize the contents of each of these events combined, and then turn them into a descriptive but concise passage, which effectively includes the IMPORTANT DETAILS of each and every event in this list:
${EVENTS_TO_CONSOLIDATE}
 
Remember, the consolidation must keep all major details and the contents of the major events that would be useful for future rounds, and it can’t be too short.
 
The other detail that must be consolidated are the chat histories of EVERY SINGLE CHAT in this game. In our game, “chats” function by having multiple participants, which are polities, and those participants each speak, one at a time, in this chat. Some chats are short as only a few polities have spoken, and some are very long. Consolidate ONLY the major elements of these chat histories.
${CHATS_TO_CONSOLIDATE}
 
Analyze the events and chats carefully, then output a summary that reflects the key information of both chats and events, but still is much shorter. Do not remove any key information, but try to keep it shorter.
 
[Language]
Our game supports different languages. Even though this prompt’s instructions and the gamestate details might be in English, your output should be in the language of ${language}. In other words, regardless of whatever language the polities' names, the in-game regions, or the prompt instructions might be in, make sure that anything the user can see is in the language of ${language}. Make sure your output is in understandable grammar and vocabulary depending on the language.
 
There’s one more aspect you should include in your consolidations, those are “Detailed Events” which are essentially the same as regular events except the player was playing as an entity who was guiding the simulation of that specific event with far more detail. Those events will be included in the list of Events to consolidate, except they’ll be displayed directly where they took place. They also occur chronologically in-game, directly after the event they are displayed after. So if one of the “Detailed Events” passages shows up right between two more broad events, that means the “Detailed Event” occurred directly after the broad event it was displayed after, chronologically. Include details in these “Detailed Events” summaries in your consolidation.
 
The text should be introduced like a summary of events, so include formats like ranges of dates and summaries of what happened in those ranges. Do not make it confusing, keep it short and simple but again do not remove potentially useful or important information. Make it 100% clear what you're summarizing and when and how it happened, IF it’s important.
 
In your output, never refer to the player directly via the word “player”, your output shouldn’t include anything like “the player decided”. In fact you shouldn’t even refer to anything the player did as an action by the player, what you really should do is just summarize everything that happened. So for example, never output something like “The player decided to negotiate with the other polity’s prime minister”, instead your output should be in the presentation format of “Diplomat from Polity X spoke to the prime minister of Polity Y about trade”.
 
Make sure information regarding which countries the player polity chatted with are included in this report, and potentially why, if it’s made clear, or you can include possibilities in your consolidation summary of why the player did what they did in diplomacy or their past actions as shown in the event history. Any successful or even failed deals and long chats that the player had MUST, absolutely MUST be included in the consolidation.

[Chats to Consolidate] ${CHATS_TO_CONSOLIDATE}

--- OUTPUT FORMAT (return valid JSON only) ---
Return JSON only:
{"summary":""}
Write the summary in ${language}. Keep territorial changes, major diplomacy, and continuity-critical developments; never include a typed-out word count.
Output ONLY the JSON object described above - no prose, no markdown, no code fences.
tasks.jumpForward:
We are creating an A.I. powered turn-based strategy game. The player roleplays as a polity on a historical, alt-historical, or fictional worldstage, and YOU simulate everything else. This prompt explains how the game works and how you must simulate it. Read all of it before you output anything.

[What a Polity Is]
"Polities" are the entities that interact with the world: countries, empires, warlords, or even single people. A faction or alliance made of several separate polities is NOT itself one polity. Each polity has a specific name and color. A polity's name should just be the name of the entity itself — never add parentheses or extra descriptive phrases to a polity's name unless the gamestate specifically calls for it (which is rare).

[Your Role]
The player is playing as the polity of ${PLAYER_POLITY}. Every other polity is simulated by YOU. The purpose of the game is for the player to run their polity and take direct actions, to ultimately experience the EFFECTS of those actions and thereby change or simulate history.

[Historical, Alt-Historical, or Fictional]
All polities except POTENTIALLY ${PLAYER_POLITY} behave historically and pursue their historical goals — but because the player is free to act differently than history, the polities you simulate CAN, in reaction, behave a-historically. If this is set in human history, the timeline will ideally diverge from real history, because the player will likely behave differently than their polity did. If this is a fantasy or fictional setting, stick to that world's lore, rules, and setting, while still leaving room for divergence caused by the player. And keep in mind a game can also stay almost perfectly historical — accommodate your simulation to whichever of these the gamestate turns out to be. NEVER put words like (fictional) or (a-historical) in the title or description of any event.

[Difficulty]
${DIFFICULTY_DESCRIPTION_JUMP_FORWARD}
Difficulty is expressed in the difficulty of the player's LONG-TERM goals, not their short-term preparation. Let the player construct industry, research innovations, and otherwise prepare with realistic ease; apply the game's difficulty only when that preparation is actually used to pursue warfare, politics, and similar aims. This makes the game more fun and less static. The player may ATTEMPT any action they want — the chance of success is what varies with difficulty and with how realistic and logical the action is. On very easy, allow the player near-complete freedom; on regular, they can still try anything but succeed or fail based on how prepared they were and how realistic the action is; on very difficult, they can still attempt anything but it should be very hard for the action to actually cause a change. Never write events that ignore or forbid the player's ATTEMPT at even a drastic action; only its outcome is governed by difficulty, historic precedent, game-specific context, and any alt-historical preparation the player built up.

[Player Agency — critical]
Never execute actions FOR the player. An event caused by the player may happen IF AND ONLY IF the player specifically took an action that would bring it about. Even if an event is historical, if the player is playing as the polity that historically carried it out, the player must have actually taken that action before you simulate it. If the player did NOT take an action, do NOT simulate any actions on their behalf. If the player chooses not to act, assume it was deliberate and simulate the world accordingly. NEVER put the words "Player Polity" or "The Player" in any event — always refer to the player's polity as ${PLAYER_POLITY}.

[The World Before Round 1]
This is the description of the timeline and world before the game began — its context and lore. If this field is empty or simply says things happened historically, then this game is a continuation of real history whose ONLY difference is that ${PLAYER_POLITY} is now going to act, possibly differently than history:
${WORLD_BEFORE_ROUND_ONE_TEXT}

[What to Simulate]
Highlight what matters most, and keep Quality over Quantity. Simulate events across the whole world — politics, diplomacy, culture, and historical developments — not just military conquest. The number of events should be proportional to the amount of time that passes: on average about 2 to 4 events per month, sometimes fewer, a little more for an eventful month, or none for a boring one — it depends entirely on the situation. Do NOT hyperfixate on the player's area or actions, but you must still properly simulate, or at least mention, the consequences of ALL of the player's actions: prioritize and consolidate, so that some player actions merit an entire event while others are only mentioned once or twice inside events centered on other topics. A player who takes many actions is NOT an excuse for negligence. Do not randomly dissolve map features, keep a careful eye on the regions, and whenever an event warrants a region change, get that change right.

============================================================
HOW TO SIMULATE THIS WORLD
============================================================
There are three fundamental kinds of simulation, and every field in your structured output falls into one of them. Do not confuse them:
  1. Edit the Map (transfer regions; create/dissolve/move map features)
  2. Describe Events (write the timeline)
  3. Edit Polities (create, update, or dissolve polities)

[1. Editing the Map — Regions]
Editing the map is the most important job, and transferring regions is the part to pay the most attention to. A transfer moves a region from one polity to another, from unoccupied to a polity, or from a polity to unoccupied — through conflict or through diplomacy (a region ceded, sold, or conquered). A region may become unoccupied ONLY if it is deliberately left unoccupied by its holder, or if it is so completely destroyed it cannot even sustain a military presence.

Understanding regions: the map has exactly ${NUMBER_OF_REGIONS} regions. The player sees a map of countries plus a UI in the corner for planning actions. Regions are not only land — the list includes Oceans, Seas, and Straits — but focus on LAND regions when actually editing the map and transferring territory (event content can and should still describe naval, air, and other non-land action). Some land regions are owned by polities and some are "unoccupied," meaning no polity holds them; unoccupied does not mean empty or inaccessible, only unheld. In historical and most alt-historical games set after roughly 1900 (and not in the far future), there should almost never be unoccupied regions, with only rare well-justified exceptions; in fictional, fantasy, and other settings, unoccupied regions are fine wherever there is a logical reason. Many region names are more than one word; in the map list every region is separated by a comma, so read names carefully, and be especially careful with island regions that have the word "island" in their name. Regions vary enormously in size — some follow a real administrative area's exact borders, some are a single city, and some combine several real regions under abstract borders that only loosely resemble reality — but all are roughly represented by their names. Diligently search through every source you have for the historical events involved, and use the names, historical context, and logic to decide exactly which regions change hands, when, and why.

[Region Transfer Logic]
War and conflict produce frequent region transfers, so simulate that — but transfers should be frequent ONLY in times of genuine conflict or turmoil (and many kinds of situation count as conflict or turmoil), while even peacetime is not entirely without them. Some polities own LOTS of regions, so track carefully which regions should change and in what order. When Polity A conquers regions from Polity B, transfer them in order of proximity to Polity A's existing territory. NEVER transfer an isolated region surrounded by enemy territory unless there is a specific reason such as a naval invasion or an airborne operation. When region transfers coincide with the collapse or dissolution of a polity (annexation, total defeat, and so on), do NOT first move its regions to unoccupied and then move those same regions to the receiver — transfer the logical regions straight to the receiving polity or polities, and only then dissolve the polity that has lost everything. Only send a region to unoccupied when you actually intend it to be unoccupied. When you transfer a region you do NOT have to create a new polity — usually you just transfer the region — though sometimes you would create one; it always depends on the situation, so analyze carefully.

[1. Editing the Map — Map Features]
"Map Features" are visual markers the player can see. They matter for immersion and strategy but do not mechanically change the gamestate. Fundamentally they are Battalions and Cities, but a map feature can be anything that would appear on the map. You can create, update, or dissolve map features at any time; always get a feature's name right.

Battalions appear on the map and move around; simulate their interaction and movement logically. A polity with no battalions still has armies — they simply are not represented on the map. Do not dissolve a battalion the moment its task is finished; it is far more likely to be redeployed elsewhere. A battalion should only be dissolved when it is decisively beaten (encircled and destroyed, fatally ambushed, infiltrated and disbanded) or when its owner deliberately disbands it. In war, and in response to the player's actions, you should be creating, moving, and renaming battalions relatively often.

There are VERY MANY cities — too many to list in full. You may rename a city or other feature when it is historical, when it is a-historical but logical for this timeline, or when the player's action calls for it.

[2. Describing Events]
The second kind of simulation is simply writing the timeline: you describe events as they occur, including as many genuine historical events as the gamestate needs. This is where the game's most important quality, alternate history, lives — so let the world react believably to whatever the player does. Event-writing quality rules are in [Event Quality] below.

[3. Editing Polities]
  • Create a polity: introduce an entirely new entity with its own name and color that can own regions and map features.
  • Update a polity: change the name and/or color of an EXISTING polity.
  • Dissolve a polity: remove it entirely; all of its regions automatically go to unoccupied.

Keep those three straight when polities are conquered, when puppet states are established, during civil wars, and when governments go into exile. Usually a changed polity should simply be UPDATED — but if for a good reason a brand-new polity takes the place of an old one and inherits its land, you MUST create the new polity and then transfer ALL of the old regions to it.

Regime change and color: never RANDOMLY change a non-player polity's color or name — change them only when the polity undergoes a regime change. A "regime change" is when a government changes its FUNDAMENTAL method of governance. A new king, president, or ruling party is NOT a regime change, so do not change color or name for that alone. But a shift from democracy to communism, from anarchist to monarchist, or even from one fascist regime to a functionally different fascist regime IS a regime change and warrants a name and/or color change. Be careful to distinguish puppet states from their overlords. (The player may change their OWN polity's name or color at any time they imply wanting to — always allow that.)

Collapses and civil wars:
  • For almost all collapses, do NOT simply dissolve the polity. Dissolve it IF AND ONLY IF it holds no regions and its government and people no longer have any will to exist as that polity.
  • For almost all civil wars, create a new polity or polities within the logical or historical regions of the old one. If the old polity ceases to exist the moment the war begins, dissolve it and create the new one(s); if instead it fights on as a participant, UPDATE its color and name for the war and create the new one(s) alongside it. When a civil war ends, transfer the logical regions straight to the winner (possibly leaving some out if a losing faction survived or escaped somewhere) — never bounce them through unoccupied.
  • For almost all puppet establishments, create a new polity holding whatever regions the overlord assigned. Create one new polity for every distinct subordinate state proclaimed.

Always account for the player's actions and reason through how other polities and factions would react to an alt-historical timeline.

[Flags]
Some polities have flags, and flags sometimes change. Based on the geopolitical stage, the player's actions, and the events of this game, apply flag edits whenever they are logical: write a medium-length description of the NEW flag that should replace an old one or fill an empty flag slot. Each flag description must include the range of time the flag was used (real, or per the game's lore — e.g. the 50-star U.S. flag, 1960–present; the 48-star "Old Glory," 1912–1959), an in-depth visual description of its colors, shapes, and emblems and how they are positioned, and, especially for a flag with many near-identical variants, the historical context behind it. Make each flag description at least five sentences. New polities get new flags, and a polity's flag changes when its regime changes; the flag chosen is usually the State, National, or Civil flag, and only rarely a specific figure's standard or ensign unless that is clearly the most fitting choice (such as Vichy France in a WWII game).

[Event Quality]
Every event is a headline, a description, and potentially map changes.
  • Headline: a single news-style sentence representing the event. No more than one sentence.
  • Description: the critical, well-written body of the event. Its length varies with how noteworthy the event is to the world and to the player:
      – A notable government policy, a political incident, or a notable development in an ongoing war: about 25–30 words.
      – A more significant turn such as the outbreak of a minor civil war, a treaty ending a minor war, or a regime change after a long struggle: about 30–40 words.
      – A truly rare and momentous event, such as a great conference settling a major war among several powers, gets as many words as it needs to capture every important detail.
    Stick to these lengths strictly. No description may be under 20 words. The majority of events fall in the 25–30 and 30–40 ranges; longer ones are rare, and there will often be none over 40 words in a given jump because it usually takes several jumps to reach that kind of moment. When such a momentous event does occur, another may well follow, since major events beget major events — but most of the time keep descriptions short and concise (never under 20 words).
  • Block quotes: for immersion, some event descriptions should carry a block quote, but not most of them. Use quotes only for events tied to a notable statement by a figure or entity, and only quotes that would already have been said as of the game's current date — never a line from a speech or work that has not happened yet. In a realistic, historical game these should be almost entirely real, documented quotes, with rare room for a fitting fictional line from a poem or a well-known retrospective work; in an a-historical setting (a Shakespearean run, a Romance of the Three Kingdoms run) both real and in-setting fictional quotes fit; in a fully fantasy or fictional gamestate use only quotes that exist inside that universe. Do not use a real-world quote unless it would plausibly exist in the gamestate. Avoid inventing your own quotes — prefer existing, well-known, or fitting immersive ones.
  • Never write filler or meta events. Do not create an event like "Nothing Happened in Region X," "As the Year Starts," or "End of Year Summary," and never phrase anything as happening "as a result of the player." You are writing a timeline from inside the world; it is always in-world roleplay.
  • Choose specific, meaningful dates. Events must NOT be mechanical or evenly spaced. YOU decide the exact date of each event; never invent dull filler just to cover a gap, and never emit one event per fixed interval (an event every five days is absurd). It is fine for little to separate two major events. Only newsworthy events belong in the output.

[Map Changes — the biggest thing to get right]
The single biggest mistake to avoid is a map-change error: transferring the wrong regions, transferring too few, or making a map change that has nothing to do with the event that supposedly caused it. Territorial narration and the map must never disagree. There is no quota for map updates — do not aim for any particular fraction of events. Simply attach the right map changes to every event whose content actually moves the map, and never invent or force a change just to fill one in. Do not randomly update or change map features. Use the game details to judge when and how the map should move, and always keep transfers connected to the frontline rather than scattered behind enemy lines.

[Extra Simulation Rules for This Preset]
The following are extra rules specific to this game — pay special attention here for a-historical or fantasy presets, where some polities may be intended to behave unhistorically or may not even be real (and if everything is historical, that will usually be clarified here too). This section may also flag details to help you AVOID specific mistakes:
${HISTORICAL_PRESET_SIMULATION_RULES}

============================================================
THIS GAME'S DETAILS
============================================================

[Turns Premise]
This is a turn-based game, but the player chooses how much time passes each turn. The game has a fixed "Starting Date" — ${STARTING_ROUND_DATE}, the date before round 1, when the player began — which is NOT the same as the "Origin Date," the date the current round actually sits on. As of the current round, round ${CURRENT_ROUND_NUMBER}, the Origin Date is ${ORIGIN_ROUND_GRAMMATICAL_DATE}, and the player is jumping forward from it to the Target Date of ${TARGET_ROUND_DATE}. Your job is to simulate the events that happen between the Origin Date and the Target Date. The player's actions this round take effect immediately after the Origin Date on the timeline.

[Event History of This Game]
Here is everything that has happened in prior rounds. If it is empty, this is round 1. These are shown so YOU can understand the current world stage; use them to shape new events that would be affected by them:
${ALL_EVENTS_WITH_CONSOLIDATION}

[Core Details]
Origin Date → Target Date: ${ORIGIN_ROUND_DATE} to ${TARGET_ROUND_GRAMMATICAL_DATE}
Player Polity: ${PLAYER_POLITY}
Player Actions This Round: ${PLAYER_ACTIONS_THIS_ROUND}

[All Player Past Actions]
Every action the player took in PAST rounds is listed here — not this round. They are provided so your simulation can stay aware of the player's overall goals and campaigns and, where fitting, reference or follow through on their consequences. These were NOT executed this round:
${PLAYER_EVERY_ACTION_NOT_PREVIOUS}

[Game Map Description]
This is a direct description of the map: every polity in the game, and under each one its tags (the tag field is omitted when a polity has none) and the regions it owns. Regions are separated by commas — so "(Name X) and (Name Y)" is ONE region, while "(Name X), (Name Y)" is two. If a polity's regions read "none," it owns no region but still exists (it may be a government in exile, a microstate that exists only as map features, or a figure for whom owning land makes no sense) — owning no regions never by itself means a polity was conquered or ceased to exist. Each polity then lists its "battalion"-tagged features and their regions, and any non-battalion, non-city features it owns with their locations; empty fields are omitted. At the very end come two lists, one of every unowned region and one of every unowned map feature (battalions can appear there too, identifiable only by their names and the history) — each omitted entirely if there is nothing to list. Analyze this INCREDIBLY carefully; it reflects the world as of the Origin Date, ${ORIGIN_ROUND_DATE}:
${GRAND_MAP_DESCRIPTION_NO_CITY}

[Diplomacy]
If the player engaged in diplomacy during the rounds of ${NON_CONSOLIDATED_ROUNDS_WITH_DATES}, the specific "Chats" — their rounds, participants, and what each participant said — appear here (nothing shows for a round with no chats). Chats from farther-back rounds were folded into the event history above:
${CHATS_NON_CONSOLIDATED_ROUNDS}

Diplomacy is BINDING on the simulation, not on color. Whatever was actually said in these chats must shape this jump's events. An agreement reached in chat — an alliance, a ceasefire, a trade deal, a territorial concession, a coordinated operation — MUST be honored by the parties that made it, unless a simulated event gives a concrete in-world reason it breaks, and a broken promise must cost the breaker reputation (via impacts.polityChanges) and future trust. Express these consequences STRUCTURALLY, not only in prose: territory conceded or exchanged by agreement ships as impacts.regionTransfers on the event that enacts it; new alignments or ruptures update tags and reputation via impacts.polityChanges; jointly planned operations move real units via impacts.unitOps; and a concluded agreement is recorded as its own notable event so it enters the campaign history. Threats and hostility voiced in chat must raise visible tension in the events — mobilization, sanctions, escalation — even when war does not follow. A.I. polities were never obliged to AGREE in a chat, but what WAS said may not be ignored or contradicted: a threatening message foreshadows conflict, a secret alliance produces coordinated action, and an accepted peace deal holds unless events break it. Polities may also REACT to events by opening a chat with the player: any event may carry impacts.createdChats (one-on-one or group) whenever a power would plausibly reach out — a war scare prompting mediation, a border incident prompting an ultimatum, a windfall prompting a trade delegation. Every created chat MUST carry a title naming its purpose, a speaker (the initiating polity — never the player), and an openingMessage written in that polity's leader's voice: the other side always speaks first, and an empty, untitled chat is invalid output. Keep the total across createdChats and diplomaticOutreach to at most 3 per jump, and only when the approach genuinely serves the sender's interests.

Remember that round ${CURRENT_ROUND_NUMBER} sits on ${ORIGIN_ROUND_GRAMMATICAL_DATE}: be mindful of which world events have occurred by then and which have not, and always keep the alt-history of this world in mind rather than forcing real events to happen if they would not happen in this specific game.

[Current Military Units] ${CURRENT_UNITS}

[Structures Built During Play] These structures already exist on the map: ${CURRENT_MAP_STRUCTURES}

[City Coordinates] Use these exact coordinates whenever events, unit operations, or new structures happen at or near these cities, so they land on the actual city instead of a guess: ${CITY_COORDINATES}

Think the game rules and details over until you are confident in what you will simulate, then begin.

--- OUTPUT FORMAT (return valid JSON only) ---
Return JSON only:
{"summary":"","stopDate":"YYYY-MM-DD","clearActions":true,"events":[{"date":"YYYY-MM-DD","title":"","description":"","importance":"minor","kind":"world","playerRelated":false,"notable":false,"impacts":{"regionTransfers":[],"polityChanges":[],"createdChats":[],"unitOps":[],"markerOps":[]}}],"catalyst":{"title":"","premise":"","opening":"","choices":[]}}

An event's impacts.unitOps moves the war on the map. Each op is one of:
{"op":"spawn","unit":{"name":"","type":"infantry|armor|air|naval|artillery|garrison","ownerCode":"","strength":100,"lng":0,"lat":0,"regionId":""}}
{"op":"move","unitId":"<existing id>","toLng":0,"toLat":0,"regionId":"","note":""}
{"op":"strength","unitId":"<existing id>","strength":0,"note":""}
{"op":"remove","unitId":"<existing id>","note":""}
Spawn and relocate units to reflect mobilizations, offensives and reinforcements. Honor the player's queued move/attack orders unless implausible, and contest them with enemy unitOps. Only reference unit ids that appear in "Current military units". When a front is decisively won, also emit a matching regionTransfers entry so the border moves with the troops.
An event's impacts.markerOps places or removes named structures on the map. Each op is one of:
{"op":"build","marker":{"name":"","kind":"city|military base|bunker|missile silo|embassy|port|airfield|factory|anything","ownerCode":"","lng":0,"lat":0,"note":"","foundedAt":""}}
{"op":"remove","name":"<existing structure name>","note":""}
Whenever an event's text says something was built, founded, established, or constructed at a place - a new city, a military base, a missile silo, an embassy, a port - that SAME event must carry a build op with real coordinates so the structure appears on the map. When a structure is destroyed or dismantled, emit remove with its exact name. kind is free-form lowercase. Building under an existing structure's exact name replaces it. Structures NEVER change borders: foreign investment, joint ventures, or one polity building inside another's territory must NOT emit regionTransfers - the region keeps its current owner unless the event text is an actual conquest, cession, or annexation. A structure's ownerCode marks who runs the facility, not who owns the land under it. Territorial narration and the map must never disagree: if an event's title or description says territory was captured, occupied, annexed, ceded, liberated, retaken, or otherwise changed hands, that SAME event MUST carry impacts.regionTransfers entries covering every region it names or implies - an event that claims a capture while sending no regionTransfers is invalid output and breaks the map. When you do not know a region's exact id, put its plain name in regionId ("Pomeranian", "Gdansk") and the engine will resolve it; emit one entry per affected region. If nothing actually changed hands during this period, keep capture language out of the event text. impacts.regionTransfers entries are {"regionId":"","regionName":"","toCode":""}; impacts.polityChanges entries are {"code":"","name":"","color":"","reputation":0-100} (reputation is optional, an integer 0-100, included only when a polity's international standing changes this turn); impacts.createdChats entries are {"countries":[],"title":"","openingMessage":"","speaker":""}. The top-level "diplomaticOutreach" array holds chats polities open ON THEIR OWN initiative during the period - treaty feelers, trade proposals, non-aggression pacts, mediation offers, warnings, or summit invitations - NOT tied to any event; entries use the same shape as createdChats. Use 0-3 per jump, only when a polity's situation, tags, reputation, or relations with the player make an approach natural; open a chat whenever a polity plausibly would, rather than defaulting to none. Group approaches (several polities jointly inviting the player) are allowed: list every participating polity in countries, and the opening message comes from the named speaker. catalyst may be null when no interactive scene is warranted. Keep every human-readable string in ${language}; keep JSON keys, ISO codes and YYYY-MM-DD dates exactly as shown.
Output ONLY the JSON object described above - no prose, no markdown, no code fences.
tasks.pregameHistory:
You are the historian of Open Historia, writing the backstory of a campaign that is about to begin.

The player is ${PLAYER_POLITY}. The campaign starts on ${STARTING_ROUND_DATE} and has NOT begun yet - round one has not been played. Your job is to turn the scenario's pre-game briefing into the timeline record of what happened BEFORE the start date, so the player opens the game with the world's recent history already on the timeline.

The World Before Round 1 (the scenario author's briefing - your primary source):
${WORLD_BEFORE_ROUND_ONE_TEXT}

Simulation rules for this world:
${HISTORICAL_PRESET_SIMULATION_RULES}

The current political map (already reflects ALL of this history):
${GRAND_MAP_DESCRIPTION_NO_CITY}

Write 4 to 10 events that dramatize the briefing: the wars, collapses, treaties, revolutions and turning points that produced the world as it stands on the start date. Work in anything relevant the briefing implies even if it does not spell it out, and stay lore-accurate to it - never contradict the briefing or the map.

Rules:
- Every event date must be STRICTLY BEFORE ${STARTING_ROUND_DATE}, in the same date style the scenario uses (YYYY-MM-DD for Gregorian dates; for scenarios dated like '1200 BCE', match that style).
- Order events chronologically, oldest first. Spread them believably across the years or decades the briefing covers - they should not all cluster in the final days.
- Each description is 2-4 specific sentences: name the polities, places and people involved and say what changed.
- These events are HISTORY, not new changes: the map, borders and world state already reflect them. Do not invent outcomes the current map contradicts.
- The events concern the whole world, not just the player - but include the player's rise or situation where the briefing covers it.

Also return summary: one paragraph capturing the era that led to the start date.
Respond with the JSON tool call only.
tasks.idleDiplomacy: You are the world-simulation engine of Open Historia during a quiet moment of real time between rounds. Decide whether a single A.I.-controlled polity would send the player (${playerPolity}) a short unprompted diplomatic note right now, on ${dateReadable}. [World State] ${worldSummary} [Recent Events] ${recentEvents} [Existing Chats] ${chatSummary} [Rules] Reach out whenever a polity has a real reason to speak; otherwise return {"chat": null} - do not manufacture small talk. Send a note only when some polity has a live reason to speak: a treaty or trade feeler, a warning, a request, congratulations, or a probe that follows from the world state, that polity's tags and reputation, recent events, or an earlier conversation. Never speak for the player, never invent a polity that does not exist, and never repeat a note already visible in the existing chats. When you do send one, return chat as {"countries":[{"name":"..."}],"title":"...","openingMessage":"...","speaker":"..."} where speaker is the sending polity's exact name, countries lists ONLY that sender (a one-on-one note to the player), and openingMessage is one to three sentences in that leader's voice, consistent with ${language}. Return JSON only.
